section.title: The Council at Jerusalem
section.sectionRationale: The open door to the Gentiles forces the church's deepest question: must a Gentile become a Jew, keeping the law of Moses and circumcision, in order to be saved? The apostles and elders gather in Jerusalem to decide. Peter, Paul, Barnabas, and James testify that God saves Jew and Gentile alike by grace through faith, and the council frees the Gentiles from the yoke of the law while asking them, for the sake of fellowship, to abstain from a few things that would gravely offend Jewish believers. It is the hinge on which the gospel of grace turns.
section.thesis: Salvation is by the grace of the Lord Jesus through faith, not by circumcision or the law of Moses, and so the church, led by the Spirit, refuses to lay the law's yoke on Gentile believers, asking of them only what love and fellowship require.
section.frameNote: Four movements: the dispute over circumcision; the council's testimony from Peter, Barnabas and Paul, and James; the letter to the Gentile believers with its four requests; and the joy at Antioch as the decision is delivered.

units.label: The dispute over circumcision
units.note: must Gentiles keep the law to be saved?

verses.text: Then some men came down from Judea and were teaching the brothers, “Unless you are {{peritemno|circumcised}} according to the custom of Moses, you cannot be saved.”
And after engaging these men in sharp debate, Paul and Barnabas were appointed, along with some other believers, to go up to Jerusalem to see the apostles and elders about this question.
Sent on their way by the church, they passed through Phoenicia and Samaria, recounting the conversion of the Gentiles and bringing great joy to all the brothers.
On their arrival in Jerusalem, they were welcomed by the church and apostles and elders, to whom they reported all that God had done through them.
But some believers from the party of the Pharisees stood up and declared, “The Gentiles must be circumcised and required to obey the law of Moses.”

units.label: Peter's testimony: saved by grace
units.note: no distinction; hearts cleansed by faith

verses.text: So the apostles and elders met to look into this matter.

After much discussion, Peter got up and said to them, “Brothers, you know that in the early days God made a choice among you that the Gentiles would hear from my lips the message of the gospel and believe.
And God, who knows the heart, showed His approval by giving the Holy Spirit to them, just as He did to us.
He made no distinction between us and them, for He cleansed their hearts by {{pistis|faith}}.
Now then, why do you test God by placing on the necks of the disciples a {{zygos|yoke}} that neither we nor our fathers have been able to bear?
On the contrary, we believe it is through the {{charis|grace}} of the Lord Jesus that we are saved, just as they are.”

units.label: James and the witness of the prophets
units.note: God rebuilds David's tent to gather the nations

verses.text: The whole assembly fell silent as they listened to Barnabas and Paul describing the signs and wonders God had done among the Gentiles through them.
When they had finished speaking, James declared, “Brothers, listen to me!
Simon has told us how God first visited the Gentiles to take from them a people to be His own.
The words of the prophets agree with this, as it is written:
‘After this I will return and rebuild the fallen tent of David. Its ruins I will rebuild, and I will restore it,
so that the remnant of men may seek the Lord, and all the Gentiles who are called by My name, says the Lord who does these things
that have been known for ages.’
It is my judgment, therefore, that we should not cause trouble for the Gentiles who are turning to God.
Instead, we should write and tell them to abstain from food polluted by idols, from sexual immorality, from the meat of strangled animals, and from blood.
For Moses has been proclaimed in every city from ancient times and is read in the synagogues on every Sabbath.”

units.label: The letter to the Gentile believers
units.note: it seemed good to the Holy Spirit and to us

verses.text: Then the apostles and elders, with the whole church, decided to select men from among them to send to Antioch with Paul and Barnabas. They chose Judas called Barsabbas and Silas, two leaders among the brothers,
and sent them with this letter: The apostles and the elders, your brothers, To the brothers among the Gentiles in Antioch, Syria, and Cilicia: Greetings.
It has come to our attention that some went out from us without our authorization and unsettled you, troubling your minds by what they said.
So we all agreed to choose men to send to you along with our beloved Barnabas and Paul,
men who have risked their lives for the name of our Lord Jesus Christ.
Therefore we are sending Judas and Silas to tell you in person the same things we are writing.
It seemed good to the Holy Spirit and to us not to burden you with anything beyond these essential requirements:
You must abstain from food sacrificed to idols, from blood, from the meat of strangled animals, and from sexual immorality. You will do well to avoid these things. Farewell.

units.label: Joy at Antioch
units.note: the churches are strengthened and rejoice

verses.text: So the men were sent off and went down to Antioch, where they assembled the congregation and delivered the letter.
When the people read it, they rejoiced at its encouraging message.
Judas and Silas, who themselves were prophets, said much to encourage and strengthen the brothers.
After spending some time there, they were sent off by the brothers in peace to return to those who had sent them.
But Paul and Barnabas remained at Antioch, along with many others, teaching and preaching the word of the Lord.

peritemno.gloss: To circumcise. The demand at issue is that Gentiles be circumcised and keep the law of Moses to be saved. The council rejects circumcision as a requirement for salvation, since a right standing comes by grace through faith, not by the covenant marker of the flesh.

pistis.gloss: Faith, trust. Peter says God cleansed the Gentiles' hearts by faith, making no distinction between Jew and Gentile. Faith, not the law, is the means by which God purifies and accepts; the whole council turns on this word.

zygos.gloss: A yoke, a heavy burden laid across the shoulders. Peter calls the law as a means of salvation a yoke that neither the fathers nor the present generation could bear. To lay it on the Gentiles would be to test God and deny the gospel of grace.

charis.gloss: Grace, unearned favour. We are saved through the grace of the Lord Jesus, just as they are. This is the decisive confession of the council: salvation is a gift received by faith, the same for Jew and Gentile, and not a wage earned by keeping the law.

basic.context: The success of the Gentile mission raises a burning question. Men from Judea come to Antioch teaching that unless Gentiles are circumcised and keep the law of Moses, they cannot be saved. Paul and Barnabas dispute this fiercely, and the matter is taken to the apostles and elders in Jerusalem. After much debate, Peter recalls how God gave the Spirit to Gentiles by faith without the law, Paul and Barnabas tell of God's work among the nations, and James shows from the prophets that God always meant to gather Gentiles. The council rules that Gentiles are not bound by the law, asking only that they abstain from a few practices that would wound fellowship with Jewish believers. A letter is sent, and the church at Antioch rejoices.
basic.hermeneutics: This is the theological turning point of Acts. Read Peter's speech as the doctrinal core: God made no distinction, cleansing hearts by faith, and we are saved through grace, not the yoke of the law. James then anchors this in Scripture, Amos 9, and offers a pastoral solution. Distinguish the two things the council settles: the truth of salvation by grace, which is permanent, and the four abstentions, which serve peace and fellowship in a mixed church.
basic.originalLanguages: The debate turns on a few decisive words. The demand is that Gentiles be circumcised, peritemno, and keep the law. Peter answers that God cleansed their hearts by pistis, faith, refusing to lay on them a zygos, a yoke, that no one could bear. The confession that settles it is charis: we are saved through the grace of the Lord Jesus, just as they are.
basic.typology: James reads the rebuilding of David's fallen tent, from Amos 9, as fulfilled in Christ, the son of David, whose risen reign gathers the remnant of Israel and the nations into one people. The Gentiles' inclusion is not an afterthought but the ancient purpose of God, foretold and now fulfilled.
basic.christ: The whole council rests on Christ. It is through the grace of the Lord Jesus that we are saved, Jew and Gentile alike. The rebuilt tent of David is the kingdom of the risen Son of David, and the door to that kingdom is opened by His grace, not by the law. To require the law for salvation would be to say Christ's grace is not enough.
basic.god: God is the one who made the choice to save Gentiles, who knows the heart, who gave the Spirit, who cleansed hearts by faith, and who long ago promised to gather the nations. The decision is not merely the church's; it seemed good to the Holy Spirit and to us. Salvation is God's gift of grace, offered on the same terms to all.
basic.crossReferences: Amos 9:11-12 is the prophecy James cites for the gathering of the Gentiles. Galatians 2 gives Paul's own account of this controversy and the gospel of justification by faith. Acts 10 records the Gentile Pentecost Peter recalls. Ephesians 2 shows Jew and Gentile made one in Christ, the wall broken down.
basic.oneStory: The council secures the gospel that drives the whole book: the ascended Christ saves the nations by grace through faith, apart from the law, and gathers Jew and Gentile into one people. Had the church bound the law on the Gentiles, the mission to the ends of the earth would have been strangled at the root. Instead the door of faith stands open, and the word keeps advancing.

context.literary: Acts 15 is the structural and theological centre of the book, resolving the tension built up since the Gentile Pentecost of chapter 10 and the mission of chapters 13 and 14. It is arranged around three speeches, Peter, Barnabas and Paul, and James, followed by the council's letter, a pattern that shows the church reaching a Spirit-led, scripturally grounded consensus.
context.historical: The council met around AD 48 to 49. The question was not merely ritual but the very nature of salvation and the unity of the church. The four abstentions correspond to practices tied to pagan temples and to the laws in Leviticus 17 and 18 that especially troubled Jewish consciences, making table fellowship between Jewish and Gentile believers possible.
context.canonical: The decision undergirds the New Testament gospel of justification by faith apart from works of the law, developed in Romans and Galatians. It also shows the early church's model for settling doctrine: by apostolic testimony, the witness of God's acts, the authority of Scripture, and the leading of the Spirit.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To record how the church settled, under the Spirit and from Scripture, that Gentiles are saved by grace through faith apart from the law of Moses, and to show the pastoral wisdom by which the freed Gentiles were asked to honour the consciences of Jewish believers for the sake of unity.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The permanent truth prescribed is that salvation is by grace through faith, not by law or circumcision. The four abstentions are best read as a pastoral provision for the peace of a mixed church in that setting; the abiding principle is that the free should gladly limit their liberty in love, as Paul teaches in Romans 14 and 1 Corinthians 8.

debates.question: Are the four prohibitions of the decree permanent moral law or a temporary pastoral provision?

views.label: A temporary pastoral bridge for fellowship
views.case: The strongest case reads the four abstentions as concessions for the sake of table fellowship in churches of mixed Jew and Gentile, tied to Leviticus 17 and 18 and to associations with pagan temples. Sexual immorality remains always sinful on other grounds, but the food restrictions serve peace, and Paul later treats food offered to idols as a matter of conscience and love, 1 Corinthians 8 to 10, not an absolute rule.

views.label: An abiding standard rooted in creation and holiness
views.case: Others hold that the four reflect enduring commands given even before Moses, and so bind the church in every age, guarding against idolatry, immorality, and the misuse of blood. Rightly held this honours the seriousness with which the decree is issued, though it must reckon with Paul's later handling of idol food as a question of conscience rather than fixed law.

debates.bearing: Held wisely, the decree teaches that the gospel frees the Gentile from the law's yoke, and that gospel freedom is exercised in love, gladly restrained where it would wound a brother or dishonour God.

debates.question: What is the relationship of law and grace that the council settles?

views.label: Salvation by grace, apart from the law
views.case: The council's own words are decisive: we are saved through the grace of the Lord Jesus, just as they are, and God cleansed their hearts by faith, making no distinction. The law was a yoke no one could bear, so it cannot be the ground of acceptance; grace received by faith is.

views.label: The law still has a rightful place
views.case: Others rightly note that the council does not abolish the law's witness, and James observes that Moses is still read every Sabbath. The law retains its role as God's word and guide, but, rightly held, this never makes law-keeping a condition of salvation, which the council expressly denies.

debates.bearing: The passage guards the heart of the gospel: sinners, Jew and Gentile, are accepted by grace through faith in Christ, not by the works of the law, even as the law keeps its honoured place as Scripture that points to Him.

study.originalLanguages: The council turns on a handful of weighted words. The demand is that Gentiles be circumcised, peritemno, and keep the law, making the covenant marker of the flesh a condition of salvation. Peter answers that God cleansed their hearts by pistis, faith, with no distinction between Jew and Gentile, and that to require the law would be to lay on them a zygos, a yoke, that neither the fathers nor the present generation could carry. The confession that resolves everything is charis: we are saved through the grace of the Lord Jesus, just as they are, so that salvation is a gift received by faith, never a wage earned by law.

typology.type: The rebuilt tent of David, Amos 9
typology.fulfillment: The fallen tent of David is raised in the risen Christ, the son of David, whose kingdom gathers the remnant of Israel and the nations into one people, fulfilling the prophets.
The outward sign gives way to the circumcision of the heart, the cleansing of the heart by faith that God works in Jew and Gentile alike, Romans 2 and Colossians 2.

typology.type: The unbearable yoke of the law
typology.fulfillment: The yoke no one could bear is answered by Christ, whose yoke is easy and whose grace saves, so that acceptance rests on Him and not on the law.

study.christ: Christ is the centre of the council's decision. It is through the grace of the Lord Jesus that both Jew and Gentile are saved, and to demand the law for salvation would deny the sufficiency of His grace. The rebuilt tent of David is the kingdom of the risen Son of David, whose reign draws the nations to God. The cleansing of the heart by faith is cleansing by union with Christ, and the freedom the council secures is the freedom the gospel of Christ creates.
study.god: God is the acting subject throughout: He chose to save the Gentiles, He knows the heart, He gave the Spirit, He cleansed hearts by faith, and He foretold long ago that He would gather the nations. The council does not invent a policy but discerns what God has done and said, so that its verdict can be issued as the mind of the Spirit: it seemed good to the Holy Spirit and to us. Salvation on the same terms for all is God's own gracious design.

crossReferences.note: The prophecy James cites: God rebuilds David's fallen tent so that the remnant and the Gentiles may seek the Lord.
Paul's own account of this controversy and his summary that a person is justified by faith in Christ, not by works of the law.
The Gentile Pentecost Peter recalls, when God gave the Spirit to Gentiles by faith, making no distinction.
Christ has made Jew and Gentile one, breaking down the dividing wall, the unity the council protects.

study.oneStory: This council is the hinge of Acts and of the church's story. The ascended Christ saves the nations by grace through faith, apart from the law, gathering Jew and Gentile into one people, exactly as the prophets foretold. Had the law's yoke been fastened on the Gentiles, the gospel's march to the ends of the earth would have stalled. Instead the door of faith is held open by grace, and Paul and Barnabas keep teaching and preaching the word of the Lord.

quiz.q: What was the question the council at Jerusalem had to settle?

options.text: Whether Gentiles must be circumcised and keep the law of Moses to be saved.
options.note: Verses 1 and 5 state the exact question the council addressed.

options.text: Whether Gentiles could be admitted to the church at all.
options.note: Close, but Gentiles were already believers; the issue was whether the law and circumcision were required for salvation.

options.text: Which leader should have authority over the churches.
options.note: The council was about the terms of salvation, not a power struggle over leadership.

options.text: Whether Paul was a genuine apostle.
options.note: Paul's apostleship was not the question; the terms of Gentile salvation were.

quiz.q: What did Peter argue at the council?

options.text: That God saves Jew and Gentile alike by grace through faith, without the yoke of the law.
options.note: Verses 9 to 11 give Peter's core argument: cleansed by faith, saved by grace.

options.text: That God had given the Spirit to Gentiles just as to the Jews.
options.note: True and part of his case, but the point he drives to is salvation by grace, not law.

options.text: That Gentiles should keep a reduced version of the law.
options.note: Peter argued against laying the law's yoke on them at all, not for a lighter version.

options.text: That circumcision was still necessary for full acceptance.
options.note: Peter argued the opposite, that the law is a yoke no one could bear and salvation is by grace.

quiz.q: How did James support the decision from Scripture?

options.text: He cited the prophets that God would rebuild David's tent to gather the Gentiles who bear His name.
options.note: Verses 16 to 17 quote Amos 9 to show the Gentile ingathering was God's ancient plan.

options.text: He agreed that God was doing a new thing among the Gentiles.
options.note: He affirmed God's work, but his key move was grounding it in the prophets, not calling it merely new.

options.text: He appealed only to the miracles Paul and Barnabas reported.
options.note: The signs were mentioned, but James rested the case on the written word of the prophets.

options.text: He argued the Gentiles must first become Jews.
options.note: James concluded the opposite, that Gentiles turning to God should not be troubled with the law.

quiz.q: Why did the council ask Gentiles to abstain from a few things?

options.text: For the sake of fellowship and peace with Jewish believers, not as a means of salvation.
options.note: The abstentions serve the unity of a mixed church; salvation had already been settled as by grace.

options.text: Because these particular practices were especially offensive to Jewish consciences.
options.note: True as far as it goes, but the deeper aim was preserving fellowship, and salvation was never in view as earned.

options.text: To require Gentiles to keep the most important parts of the law for acceptance.
options.note: The requests were not conditions of acceptance; the council had just freed them from the law's yoke.

options.text: To make the Gentiles prove their sincerity before being saved.
options.note: Salvation was by grace through faith; the abstentions were about love and fellowship, not proving worth.

quiz.q: What does the council teach about salvation?

options.text: That Jew and Gentile alike are saved through the grace of the Lord Jesus, received by faith.
options.note: Verse 11 is the confession at the heart of the council: saved through grace, just as they are.

options.text: That God accepts Gentiles because He gave them the Spirit.
options.note: The Spirit's gift is evidence, but the ground stated is the grace of the Lord Jesus received by faith.

options.text: That grace gets a person started but the law must complete them.
options.note: The council rejected adding the law; salvation is by grace through faith from start to finish.

options.text: That salvation is earned by keeping the law of Moses.
options.note: This is precisely what the council denied, calling the law a yoke no one could bear.

quiz.q: How was the decision described when the letter was written?

options.text: It seemed good to the Holy Spirit and to us.
options.note: Verse 28 shows the council understood its verdict as the mind of the Spirit, not mere human opinion.

options.text: The apostles and elders agreed together after much discussion.
options.note: True, they did agree after debate, but the letter grounds it above all in the Holy Spirit's leading.

options.text: The majority voted and the minority submitted.
options.note: The text stresses Spirit-led consensus and shared agreement, not a bare majority vote.

options.text: Paul decided the matter on his own authority.
options.note: The decision was the whole council's, attributed to the Holy Spirit, not to Paul alone.

questions: The false teaching added circumcision and law-keeping to grace. Where are you tempted to add conditions to the gospel, as if grace alone were not enough?
Peter called the law a yoke no one could bear. What burdens are you carrying to try to earn a standing with God that Christ offers you by grace?
The council grounded its decision in Scripture, testimony, and the Spirit's leading. How does your church discern hard questions, and what could you learn from this pattern?
The freed Gentiles were asked to limit their liberty for the sake of fellowship. Where might love call you to give up a freedom so as not to wound another believer?
The letter brought great joy and encouragement to the church. How does the truth that you are saved by grace, not by your performance, bring you fresh joy today?

translationNotes.note: James quotes Amos 9:11-12, following the wording of the Greek Old Testament, to show that the gathering of the Gentiles was foretold by the prophets.

The four abstentions, from idol food, sexual immorality, strangled meat, and blood, echo the laws for foreigners in Leviticus 17 and 18 and safeguard table fellowship between Jewish and Gentile believers.

The BSB omits verse 34, going from verse 33 to verse 35, because the earliest and best manuscripts do not contain it. A later addition, reflected in the Textus Receptus and noted in the BSB footnote, reads that Silas decided to remain there; it is not part of the original text, so no verse 34 appears in this study.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Acts 15. Passage covers 15:1-35; verse 34 is omitted by the BSB (absent from the earliest manuscripts) and is carried faithfully as an omission, noted in translationNotes. Footnote markers, line breaks, and poem or note objects were skipped, keeping only text and joining elements with single spaces. No em or en dashes appear. Sermon left as none; no expositor sermon verified this session.
